title: Generic function
instruction:
A function with `<T>` works for any type.

Run this.
successMessage: The same function works for &[i32] and &[&str]. T is a placeholder that the compiler fills in based on usage.

title: Bounded generics
instruction:
Add trait bounds to restrict which types are accepted:

Run this.
successMessage: T: PartialOrd means 'T must support >'. Without the bound, the compiler cannot use > on T.

title: Generic struct
instruction:
Structs can be generic over one or more types.

Run this.
successMessage: impl<T: ...> Pair<T> adds methods only when T satisfies the bounds. Types that don't implement Display + PartialOrd cannot call larger().

title: Multiple type parameters
instruction:
Use multiple type parameters: `<T, U>`

Run this.
successMessage: K and V are independent type parameters. The struct adapts to whatever types you use — no runtime overhead.

title: Generic enum
instruction:
Option<T> and Result<T,E> are generic enums from the standard library. Write your own.

Run this.
successMessage: Maybe<T> is essentially Option<T> reimplemented. Writing it yourself makes clear why Option is designed the way it is.

title: Zero-cost abstraction
instruction:
Generic code compiles to the same machine code as hand-written type-specific code.

Run this.
successMessage: The compiler generates three separate add functions — one per type. No boxing, no vtable, no runtime cost. This is Rust's zero-cost abstraction promise.

title: impl Trait in function position
instruction:
`impl Trait` as a parameter type is shorthand for a generic bound.

Run this.
successMessage: impl Iterator<Item=i32> means 'some type that is an Iterator producing i32'. Both Vec::IntoIter and Range implement it.

title: Phantom types
instruction:
Sometimes you want a type parameter for type safety without storing the value.

Run this.
successMessage: PhantomData marks the type parameter without storing data. Id<User> and Id<Post> are different types even though the inner value is the same u64.
